text: We present a video on the uses of ICG fluorescence imaging technology in complex inflammatory bowel disease surgery. We have no conflict of interest. We use this technology with patients who have a high-risk anastomosis, meaning they are on steroids, biologics, or have multiple segmental resections. And the success of that anastomosis may mean the difference between a permanent ileostomy or not.

The surgeon performs ICG fluorescence imaging on the rectal stump of a 31-year-old male patient with fulminant ulcerative colitis to assess tissue viability before implanting it into the subcutaneous tissues. The bowel is attached to the fascia to prevent pelvic sepsis, ensuring no ischemia occurs in the diseased bowel left in situ.

The surgeon performs a complex resection on a young male with fistulizing Crohn's disease, addressing multiple enterocolic and entero-entero fistulas and a perforation sealed by the omentum. The thickened Crohn's mesentery is divided using interlocking number one chromic sutures. Indocyanine green (ICG) is injected to assess the viability of the end-to-side stapled ileocolic anastomosis.

Our third patient had complex fistulizing ileocolic disease and progressed through medical therapy. He was taken to the operating room for a complex resection. We performed ICG to assure the viability of our ileocolic anastomosis. We also then created an omental pedicle graft. He was discharged on post-operative day ten after we medically managed his high output ileostomy.

The surgeon untwists the large dilated J-pouch, addressing the outlet obstruction and pseudo twist. Pouch augmentation is performed, followed by the injection of ICG to ensure viability. The scar at the distal end is excised, and a hand-sewn anastomosis is completed.

The surgeon assesses the viability of the J-pouch in a twenty-year-old male with a history of obesity and ulcerative colitis after performing pouch lengthening procedures. The pouch reach to the pelvis is visually confirmed, with no issues observed in blood supply.

In conclusion, although recent research has been specifically focused on ICG with colorectal anastomosis, we have demonstrated additional applications of ICG technology specifically for the IBD population.
